outlaw dark patterns that induce gambling addiction and enforce mandatory bright patterns meant specifically to prevent gambling addiction
(the concepts of gachas, lootboxes, packages and in-game currencies are known even to the part of the public that doesnt play videogames), mainly in the form of microtransactions.
dark patterns that make use of information gathered, both lawfully and not, on the user
all forms of softwares, like physical copies, even if bought in the form of a license, should remain usable to the buyer regardless of the whims of the distributors
end of life of the product is not specified at the time of purchase

paragraphs.text: I will divide my feedback in 2 main parts: one related to the ownership of a digital product and the other concerning the current state of aggressive monetization of digital products. First of all, all forms of softwares, like physical copies, even if bought in the form of a license, should remain usable to the buyer regardless of the whims of the distributors, especially in the cases where an end of life of the product is not specified at the time of purchase and/or the product includes microtransactions. We are at an age where many digital products require mandatory, yet unnecessary, online access (often due to reasons that go in direct violation with the GDPR act) with the consequence of the product becoming unusable the moment its support ends. A product should be usable offline by design not only because its a customer right to keep using a bought license for personal uses bound only by the laws and not the whim of corporations, but also because its preposterous to assume (and consequently force) that everyone is constantly, and reliably, with an active online connection. Moreover, the issue lies not only in the distributors, but in the entire chain of commerce which includes payment processors. The recent events have shown that payment processors can currently arbitrarily deprive people of the content and products they buy for reasons unrelated to the legality of said products. This sets dangerous precedents in terms of, respectively, censorship towards the artists/producers and extortion towards the customers; a problem that needs to be addressed swiftly and thoroughly in order to ensure that payment processors cannot pressure distributors into removing LEGAL content. Regarding the second part, it is imperative that gambling sites are held to the same standards, controls and penalties as physical gambling devices (like slot machines). No exception should be made on the argument for videogames, many of which are notorious for their aggressive monetization methods (the concepts of gachas, lootboxes, packages and in-game currencies are known even to the part of the public that doesnt play videogames), mainly in the form of microtransactions. Such systems and the algorithms related to said gambling are, at best, ambiguous and, at worst, specifically designed to induce gambling addiction into the user, especially minors (it is also to note that many gambling sites and games even specifically target minors), by implementing dark patterns that make use of information gathered, both lawfully and not, on the user. The ideal solution would be to mandate every algorithm related to gambling (including the aforementioned forms of gambling-based monetisation of games) to be open-source and subject to periodic control; although explicitly showing the exact numerical probabilities of any possible result offer a good equilibrium between efficacy and feasibility. What should be however absolutely mandatory, especially for the safety of children and elderly (a way that would actually protect the children instead of mass-surveillance Orwellian laws), would be to outlaw dark patterns that induce gambling addiction and enforce mandatory bright patterns meant specifically to prevent gambling addiction.
